findings.title: Plaintext secret values may leak into version control or logs
findings.description: Task 1 (subtask 1002) mentions creating secrets with 'dev placeholder values' and sealed-secrets as a fallback. If external-secrets is not available and sealed-secrets is not properly configured, there is risk of plaintext secret values being committed to the repository or stored in Helm values files. The task description says 'Do NOT commit plaintext secret values' but provides no enforcement mechanism (e.g., pre-commit hooks, gitguard scanning).
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask or gate requiring: (1) a pre-commit hook that scans for base64-encoded secrets in YAML files, (2) validation that values-dev.yaml never contains secret values (only secret resource names), (3) if sealed-secrets is used, ensure only the encrypted manifest is committed. Consider adding kubesec or gitleaks scanning as a CI step.

findings.title: PM server ServiceAccount has read access to all secrets in namespace
findings.description: Subtask 1004 grants the sigma-1-pm-server ServiceAccount get/list on ALL secrets in the sigma-1-dev namespace. This is overly broad — the PM server only needs to read sigma-1-secrets, not every secret in the namespace. If other secrets are created in the namespace (e.g., TLS certs, other service credentials), the PM server would have access to them all.
findings.recommendation: Scope the RBAC to a specific secret by name using resourceNames field in the Role: `resourceNames: ['sigma-1-secrets']` for secrets. Keep configmaps broader if needed, or similarly scope to `sigma-1-infra-endpoints`. This follows least-privilege principle.

findings.title: No input validation on PRD input or API endpoints
findings.description: Tasks 2 and 3 process PRD input through the task generation pipeline and send task context to external APIs (Hermes, Linear, GitHub). There is no mention of input validation, sanitization, or size limits on the PRD input. A malicious or malformed PRD could trigger injection attacks in downstream systems (Linear issue descriptions, GitHub PR bodies, Discord messages) or cause resource exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Add input validation subtasks: (1) validate and sanitize PRD input before processing (max length, allowed characters, strip potential markdown injection), (2) sanitize task descriptions before embedding in Linear API calls, GitHub commit content, and Discord messages, (3) implement request body size limits on the PM server API endpoints.

findings.title: GITHUB_TOKEN scope and permissions not specified
findings.description: The GITHUB_TOKEN is used in Task 4 to create branches, commit files, and create PRs in the 5dlabs/sigma-1 repository. The required token scope/permissions are not specified. An overly-scoped token (e.g., full repo access) presents risk if compromised. The token may also have org-level access beyond the single repository.
findings.recommendation: Document the minimum required GitHub token permissions: `contents: write` and `pull_requests: write` scoped to only the `5dlabs/sigma-1` repository. Use a GitHub App installation token or fine-grained PAT rather than a classic PAT. Add a comment in the secret definition specifying required scopes.

findings.title: No dependency auditing or pinning strategy defined
findings.description: Tasks 2-9 introduce Bun/Elysia (PM server), React/Next.js (frontend), and various npm dependencies (react-markdown, date-fns, potentially Octokit, SWR/React Query). There is no task for dependency version pinning, lockfile management, or vulnerability auditing. Container base images are also not specified.
findings.recommendation: Add a cross-cutting task or subtask for: (1) ensure bun.lockb and package-lock.json are committed and used in CI builds, (2) run `bun audit` or `npm audit` in CI pipeline, (3) specify container base images from trusted registries (e.g., `oven/bun:1.x-alpine` for PM server, `node:20-alpine` for frontend) with specific version tags, not `latest`, (4) integrate Snyk, Trivy, or similar scanner for container image scanning.

findings.title: Network policies allow broad HTTPS egress from PM server
findings.description: Subtask 10007 acknowledges that IP-restricting external API egress may not be feasible and suggests allowing 'all egress on port 443 from PM server only'. This effectively makes the PM server's egress policy very permissive — it can reach any HTTPS endpoint on the internet. A compromised PM server pod could exfiltrate data to arbitrary destinations.
findings.recommendation: Where possible, use FQDN-based egress policies (some CNI plugins like Cilium support this) to restrict to api.github.com, api.linear.app, and the Hermes API domain. If FQDN policies aren't available, document the risk and add compensating controls: (1) log all egress traffic from PM server pods, (2) alert on unexpected egress destinations, (3) consider using an egress proxy.

findings.title: No application-level security event logging defined
findings.description: While Kubernetes audit logging is covered in Task 10 (subtask 10008), there is no mention of application-level security event logging in the PM server or frontend. Events such as failed API key authentication, rate limiting, unusual request patterns, or failed authorization checks are not captured.
findings.recommendation: Add structured security event logging in the PM server: (1) log all external API calls with destination, status code, and duration, (2) log authentication/authorization events (API key usage, missing tokens), (3) log rate-limit events, (4) emit security events to a dedicated log stream or label them for easy filtering. Integrate with the cluster's logging stack for alerting.

findings.title: No API authentication between PM server and frontend
findings.description: Task 6 and subsequent frontend tasks fetch data from the PM server API without any mention of API authentication. While Cloudflare Access (Task 10) protects the external ingress, internal pod-to-pod communication between the frontend and PM server appears to be unauthenticated. Any pod in the namespace could call the PM server API.
findings.recommendation: Consider implementing a lightweight API key or service mesh mTLS for internal service-to-service communication. At minimum, the NetworkPolicy (10007) restricting which pods can reach the PM server provides some protection, but adding a shared API key or using Kubernetes service account token authentication adds defense in depth.

findings.title: Research memo content stored and displayed without sanitization
findings.description: Task 3 fetches research memo content from the Hermes API and stores it verbatim (raw content). Task 7 renders this content as markdown in the frontend using react-markdown. If the Hermes API returns content containing malicious HTML or scripts, react-markdown could render it as-is (though react-markdown does sanitize by default). The trust boundary with the Hermes API is not explicitly defined.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly configure react-markdown to disallow raw HTML (`allowedElements` or `disallowedElements` props). Add server-side sanitization of research memo content before storage. Document the trust boundary with the Hermes API and whether its responses are considered trusted or untrusted input.

findings.title: Connectivity test pod uses busybox/curl without image pinning
findings.description: Subtask 1006 deploys a temporary connectivity test Job using a busybox/curl container. The image tag is not specified, which means it would pull `latest` by default, introducing a supply chain risk for the test infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Pin the connectivity test image to a specific digest or version tag (e.g., `curlimages/curl:8.5.0` or `busybox:1.36.1`). Set `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` and use a trusted registry. This is low risk since it's a temporary test pod, but good hygiene.

findings.title: No pod security standards or security context defined
findings.description: None of the deployment tasks specify pod security contexts (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation, capabilities dropping). Task 10 covers resource limits and probes but not pod-level security hardening. Running containers as root is a common attack vector.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask to configure security contexts on all deployments: `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true`, `securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` (with emptyDir volumes for temp data), `securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, `securityContext.capabilities.drop: [ALL]`. Also consider enforcing Pod Security Standards at the namespace level via labels (`pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`).

findings.title: Secret rotation annotations are informational only, no automated rotation
findings.description: Subtask 10009 sets external-secrets refresh interval to 24h and adds annotations for rotation alerting, but there is no actual automated secret rotation mechanism. If a secret (e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN, LINEAR_API_KEY) is compromised, rotation requires manual intervention.
findings.recommendation: Document the manual secret rotation procedure for each secret type. Consider implementing automated rotation for secrets that support it (e.g., GitHub App tokens can be auto-rotated). Add a runbook for emergency secret rotation procedures including: revoke old credentials, generate new ones, trigger external-secrets sync, verify applications recover.

strengths: RBAC is implemented with namespace-scoped Roles (not ClusterRoles) following least-privilege principles, with explicit verification tests using kubectl auth can-i

Graceful degradation is well-designed throughout — Hermes API failures, GitHub API failures, and notification bridge failures all degrade gracefully without pipeline failure, reducing denial-of-service attack surface
Cloudflare Access for authentication (per D7) is a strong choice — SSO/MFA at the edge layer avoids implementing auth in application code, reducing auth-related vulnerability surface area
NetworkPolicies with default-deny egress are planned, which is a strong baseline for namespace isolation
Secret management uses external-secrets operator or sealed-secrets rather than plaintext secrets, with explicit instructions to never commit plaintext values
The connectivity test pod validates infrastructure before deploying application workloads, enabling early detection of network misconfigurations
Health and readiness probes are specified with concrete intervals, enabling Kubernetes to detect and remediate compromised or hung pods
summary: The plan demonstrates solid infrastructure security fundamentals with namespace-scoped RBAC, default-deny NetworkPolicies, Cloudflare Access authentication, and sealed/external secrets management. However, there are medium-severity gaps in input validation across the pipeline (PRD input, API responses rendered as markdown), overly broad secret access in RBAC (get/list all secrets rather than named resources), missing pod security contexts (runAsNonRoot, capability dropping), and no dependency auditing or container image scanning strategy. Addressing these gaps — particularly input sanitization, RBAC resource name scoping, and pod security standards — would significantly reduce the attack surface before production deployment.
